posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
Surprising results at a 2017 Bouchard and Fevre tasting last night. Based on what I had read about the vintage, I expected to like the white wines more than the reds. Turned out to be the complete opposite, I enjoyed almost every red wine served, and found the whites to be unenjoyable at this stage.

Evidently Bouchard changed their labels in the 2016 vintage, and I haven’t had any of those, so my first time seeing them tonight. In addition to changing the labels, they have dialed down the oak – though it wasn’t clear from the staff in attendance what vintage this lesser oak regimen began.

Fevre wines were all in DIAM which certainly should help given their poor track record for oxidized wines. I was advised that switch was made in 2010, I guess it’s been a while since I have bought Fevre because of this experience.

Perhaps the whites just weren’t in a good spot at the moment, or maybe it’s what Bouchard and Fevre did in the vintage, I really don’t know for sure. However, when I was in Burgundy last summer, I did taste some 2016’s that I quite enjoyed. I’ll be curious to get some data points from others on their experiences with 2017 whites and reds.

Lot of wines served, I just typed notes from memory after the tasting on the wines I especially liked, and a couple I especially disliked.

2017 BOUCHARD TASTING - Phoenix, AZ USA (3/6/2019)

  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Chambertin-Clos de Bèze - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambertin-Clos de Bèze Grand Cru
    Head and shoulders above the rest of the portfolio tonight, tremendous density. However already showing some backwardness and tannin. This is the wine to buy for the future, several other wines in the ‘17 Bouchard lineup better in the short term.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Volnay 1er Cru Caillerets Ancienne Cuvée Carnot - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Volnay 1er Cru
    One of my favorites tonight in the extensive tasting of ‘17 Bouchard. Round and delicious with just a bit more texture and nuance than the Beaune 1er wines - but splitting hairs. Presumably will shut down, for the moment eminently enjoyable.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Grèves Vigne de L’Enfant Jesus - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Beaune 1er Cru
    Delicious now and promising for the future. This has everything the delicious Clos de la Mousse had, but more. More finesse, more depth, and more gulpability. Easy to recommend, but if you’re looking for a deal Mousse was half the price and almost as good.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Clos de la Mousse - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Beaune 1er Cru
    Thought this was “the one” until I had the Baby Jesus. This is terrific, round, supple and a serious bargain. Jesus is better, but Mousse the better value, at least at today’s pricing.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Les Cailles - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru
    Huge lineup of ‘17 Bouchard tonight, and this was the only one that struck me as bitter on the finish.

Under most circumstances I’d say it just needs time, but all the other wines showed so well that this just wouldn’t be worth recommending.

  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Pommard - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Pommard
    Juicier than one might expect from a baby Pommard, but not as snowy as the Beaune 1er wines, not the Savgny Les Beaunes. Decent, but outshined tonight by other wines in the port.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Corton-Le Corton - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton Grand Cru
    If you can find a better Grand Cru red Burgundy for this price, back up the truck.

This was a noteworthy wine tonight bridging the gap between many accessible 1ers and the brooding beast of Beze.

Ripe with some dark intensity on the finish, enjoyable today likely to shut down soon, and re-emerge as a beauty in a decade or two.


  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Corton-Charlemagne - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Corton-Charlemagne Grand Cru
    Expected to like ‘17 whites more than reds based on the buzz, but I found the reverse to be true tonight in Bouchard’s portfolio. This was the best of the white wines, but not a wow. Giving the benefit of the doubt this will mature into something interesting, but honestly I’m totally rethinking ‘17 whites after the stomping they got from the reds tonight.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Meursault Les Clous - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Meursault
    Young of course, but this really put me off. Had a new world Sauvignon Blanc nose, and nothing on the palate to bring me around. None of the white wines wowed me tonight, and this was my least favorite.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Puligny-Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet
    An “ok” wine in a lineup of disappointing white wines. Decent minerality but not much grip.
  • 2017 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Clos St. Landry Blanc - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Beaune 1er Cru
    Not bad but not a standout. To be fair so young at this stage, but I’ll be more interested in Bouchard reds then whites after today’s tasting.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: Thanks for the comments. I had a 2016 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Clos de la Mousse at a tasting recently, that quite impressed me and I wanted to try again. I thought for $50 it was pretty good QPR, and will probably buy a couple. I thought some may find it too plush, but I thought it was plush but balanced and a great crowd pleasure burgundy. I plan on hitting Bouchard at Paulee this weekend, to taste that again before purchasing. Are you under the impression that 2017 had further stylistic changes in the reds since 2016?

posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
I haven’t tasted any 2016 Bouchard, so regrettably I can’t comment on the differences between the vintages at Bouchard.

The general impression I had from other 2016 reds I’ve tasted is that they are dense, and it wouldn’t surprise me if many of them are long lived. Last night’s wines struck me as more accessible, and perhaps not as long lived. But 2017 wines weren’t weak at all, and no greenness, just happy and easy to drink with good texture. If they don’t shut down, 2017 reds will probably be a fantastic vintage to re-buy many times over.

Thanks for the comments. I had a 2016 Bouchard Père et Fils Beaune 1er Cru Clos de la Mousse at a tasting recently, that quite impressed me and I wanted to try again. I thought for $50 it was pretty good QPR, and will probably buy a couple. I thought some may find it too plush, but I thought it was plush but balanced and a great crowd pleasure burgundy. I plan on hitting Bouchard at Paulee this weekend, to taste that again before purchasing. Are you under the impression that 2017 had further stylistic changes in the reds since 2016?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Evidently Bouchard changed their labels in the 2016 vintage

And, quite annoyingly, their bottles - or at least some. I haven’t figured out the formula yet, but some of their more “special” bottlings are now in larger, heavier bottles; like Jesus and other monopoles, probably their GCs, though I haven’t seen any of those yet.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:

Saw a '16 Volnay Carnot in the big heavy bottle yesterday. Stupid.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Surprising regarding the whites Alex. I have loved what I’ve seen from '17 thus far.

The Meursault Les Clous from Bouchard is a wine I buy every year from Bouchard. It is a very cool Meursault Lieu Dit and often has a Chablis-like stoniness to me. Just wondering if your bottle showed some juvenile esther characters that may settle with a few more months in bottle?

posts.name: Alex Goldstein
posts.cooked:
Honestly Jeremy I really don’t know why they showed so underwhelmingly. It certainly was surprising.

I’m usually a very tough critic on young red Burgundy and these were delicious.

My 2 friends had similar impresssions preferring the reds to whites, but one of my friends liked them enough to buy both whites and reds.

In the end this is just one persons experience on one night and in a few months could show totally differently. It will certainly be interesting to check in on them again in a year.

Surprising regarding the whites Alex. I have loved what I’ve seen from '17 thus far.

The Meursault Les Clous from Bouchard is a wine I buy every year from Bouchard. It is a very cool Meursault Lieu Dit and often has a Chablis-like stoniness to me. Just wondering if your bottle showed some juvenile esther characters that may settle with a few more months in bottle?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I’m tasting through the bottles Bouchard 2017s this week, having tasted them from barrel / tank in December of last year. My impressions are much more positive than yours, Alex, though I think the wines are quite shut down after bottling and in some cases showing their SO2. But, allowing for that, they are very consistent with how they tasted before they were bottled.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Surprising regarding the whites Alex. I have loved what I’ve seen from '17 thus far.

The Meursault Les Clous from Bouchard is a wine I buy every year from Bouchard. It is a very cool Meursault Lieu Dit and often has a Chablis-like stoniness to me. Just wondering if your bottle showed some juvenile esther characters that may settle with a few more months in bottle?

The Clous is in my glass right now. This bottle is showing aromas of pear, yellow citrus, dried white flowers and beeswax. Medium to full-bodied, ample and incisive, a bit shut down after bottling, with the SO2 surpassing a bit of the wine’s texture, concluding with an elegantly chalky, mouthwatering finish (with a bit of back-end grip from SO2). It’s under DIAM 10 and should show very well in just a couple of years.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked: Spoke with winemaker briefly at Paulee. He stated they started making changes in 2013, without significant changes to 2017 versus 2016. It was difficult to hear the changes made re oak, but I gathered they changed toast levels, and potentially the barrels themselves.
